[METADATA]
key: RHMJWZX8
doi: 10.1111/jphp.12481
title: Polysorbate 80-coated PLGA nanoparticles improve the permeability of acetylpuerarin and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats

[SYNTHESIS_METHOD_BLOCK]
Experimental design: varying concentrations or batches. Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs AP-PLGA-NPs were prepared by a solvent diffusion meth- odology. Brieﬂy, 18 mg of PLGA and 7 mg of AP were dis- solved in 1 ml of acetone to form an organic phase; then the organic phase was poured into 4 ml of stirred aqueous phase (containing 1% polysorbate 80). The formed O/W emulsion was stirred continuously at 800 rpm at 40°C for 12 h with a magnetic stir bar, and the ﬁnal NP suspension was subsequently ﬁltered through a membrane (0.8-μm pore size) to remove the non-incorporated AP.[27,28] The preparation method was optimized with a homo- geneous design combined with response surface methodol- ogy. The PLGA concentration, the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the oil phase and the AP feeding amount were selected as the factors and were evaluated according to U*12 (1210) homogeneous design. The encapsulation efﬁ- ciency (EE) and drug loading (DL) of AP-PLGA-NPs, which Puerarin: R=H Acetylpuerarin: R=CH3CO- OR OR O RO RO OR OR O O Figure 1 Chemical structure of puerarin and acetylpuerarin.

[OPTIMIZATION_RESULT_BLOCK]
Optimization result: best/highest/efficiency/loading outcome. surface, the NPs mimicked the LDL particles to interact with the LDL receptors on the BCECs, then uptaken by the cells via the receptor-mediated endocytosis. This mecha- nism was supported by lots of studies in vitro and in vivo.[25,45,51] Previous studies have indicated the protective effects of AP against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in animals. It can reduce the damage by oxygen-free radicals, increase the activity of antioxidase, decrease the calcium concentration in neurons, inhibit apoptosis of nerve cells and prevent the inﬂammatory response in the ischaemic area in vivo.[8–10] In this study, the neurological deﬁcit scores and infarct volume signiﬁcantly decreased, and the brain inﬁltration of inﬂammatory cells and neuronal pyknosis and karyolysis were signiﬁcantly alleviated 24 h after reperfusion in the rats treated with AP-PLGA-NPs. In addi- tion, AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly mitigated the apoptosis in nerve cells after cerebral ischaemic injury. AP can be metabolized to penta-acetylpuerarin, tetra-acetylpuerarin and PUE via stepwise deacetylation in vivo, and PUE is found to be the major metabolite.[27,52] When AP-PLGA-NPs were transported across the BBB, the released AP in the Figure 9 Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on the apoptosis in the cortical neurons in rats subjected to ischaemia-reperfusion injury. (a) Representative micrographs of ischaemic penumbra cortex by TUNEL staining. The TUNEL-positive cells were stained in brown. The scale bar is 20 μm. (b) Bar graph illustrating the percentage of apoptotic cells in the ischaemic penumbra cortex. Acetylpuerarin formulations signiﬁcantly reduced the number of TUNEL-positive cells 24 h after reperfusion, and more signiﬁcant reduction in the number of TUNEL-positive cells was observed in AP-PLGA-NPs group. ++P < 0.01 vs sham group; **P < 0.01 vs IR group; ##P < 0.01 vs AP group. Acetylpuerarin solution (15 mg/kg); AP-PLGA-NPs (15 mg/kg). PLGA-NPs improve AP permeability Deqing Sun et al. © 2015 Royal Pharmaceutical Society, Journal of Pharmacy and Pharmacology, 67, pp. 1650–1662 Sham IR AP AP-PLGA-NPs % of apoptotic cells Sham IR AP AP-PLGA-NPs (a) (b) ** ## ++ ** 20 μm 20 μm 20 μm 20 μm

[PARAGRAPH_BLOCK]
The NPs were characterized. The pharmacokinetics, tissue distributions and brain-protective effects of AP-PLGA- NPs were evaluated in animals. Key ﬁndings AP-PLGA-NPs were successfully prepared with a mean particle size of 145.0 nm and a zeta potential of −14.81 mV. The in-vitro release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed a biphasic release proﬁle.

[SYNTHESIS_METHOD_BLOCK]
Optimization result: best/highest/efficiency/loading outcome. Compared with AP solution, PLGA-NPs provided higher AUC, MRT, t1/2, but lower plasma clearance (CL) for both AP and PUE, indicating an increased drug exposure in blood circulation. Following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA-NPs, the AUC0−∞values of AP and PUE were 5398.60 ± 724.77 and 15106.98 ± 3652.73 h ng/ml, which were 2.90- and 2.29-fold as great as those of AP solution, respectively. The Cmax values of AP and PUE were similar in the two groups, suggesting a sustained release of the NP preparation. Figure 2 The schematic diagram (a) showing the preparation of AP-PLGA-NPs and the photograph (b) of the prepared nanoparticles under trans- mission electron microscopy.
